title: Restriction Estimates Using Decoupling Theorem and Incidence Estimates For Tubes
speaker: Hong Wang
abstract:
Suppose f is a function with Fourier transform supported on the unit sphere in Rd. Elias Stein conjectured in the 1960s that the Lp norm of f is bounded by the Lp norm of its Fourier transform, for any p greater than 2d/(d−1).  We propose to study this conjecture using Bourgain-Demeter decoupling theorem and incidence estimates for tubes. 
In this talk, we will describe a geometric conjecture, the two-ends Furstenberg conjecture, that would imply Stein's restriction conjecture. We prove it in R2 and obtain a partial result in R3 that implies the restriction estimate in R3 for any p greater than 3+1/7. This restriction estimate implies Wolff's hairbrush Kakeya bound: any Kakeya set in R3 has Hausdorff dimension at least 5/2.

proof_stages.label: Restriction conjecture and Kakeya motivation
proof_stages.summary: The speaker defines the paraboloid extension operator, states the restriction conjecture, recalls its connection to Kakeya-type dimension bounds, and frames a geometric two-ends/Furstenberg conjecture as a route toward both restriction and Kakeya.

proof_stages.label: Wave-packet reduction to tubes
proof_stages.summary: On a large ball, the extension operator is decomposed into wave packets associated with radius-R^{1/2} tubes, so estimating the L^p norm is reduced to understanding tube overlap together with oscillatory cancellation.

proof_stages.label: Refined decoupling as the oscillation input
proof_stages.summary: A refined decoupling theorem is stated for a union of R^{1/2}-cubes with a controlled number of tubes through each cube, converting the analytic restriction estimate into an incidence estimate relating the set of cubes, tube multiplicities, and the total tube family.

proof_stages.label: Discretized tube-shading incidence problem
proof_stages.summary: After rescaling to delta-tubes with delta-separated directions, the needed incidence problem is formulated as a lower bound for the volume of the union of shaded subsets of the tubes.

proof_stages.label: Two-ends nonconcentration condition
proof_stages.summary: The naive shaded-tube incidence problem is shown to be ill-posed because shadings may cluster, leading to the two-ends hypothesis that each shaded tube must not concentrate too much in any smaller ball at scales between delta and 1.

proof_stages.label: Two-ends Furstenberg set conjecture
proof_stages.summary: The central geometric conjecture is stated: Kakeya-type families of delta-tubes with two-ends shadings should satisfy a strong lower bound for the union of the shadings, a statement strong enough to imply Kakeya and tied to the classical Furstenberg set problem.

proof_stages.label: Known results and restriction consequences
proof_stages.summary: The speaker reports that the conjectural incidence statement is proved in two dimensions using Furstenberg-set estimates, gives partial three-dimensional progress leading to the best-known restriction estimate p>3+1/4, and notes further numerical improvements in higher dimensions.

examples.name: Constant function on the unit disk
examples.note: The function equal to 1 on the frequency disk is mentioned as the standard test explaining the critical exponent in the restriction conjecture, but it is not used to build the later argument.
examples.engagement_reason: The constant function on the unit disk is cited only as the example explaining where the conjectural exponent comes from, without deriving or computing it in the talk.

passes.reason: the full-disk constant datum is maximally rotationally regular and is used as the necessary-example obstruction to estimates below the conjectured p-range
The indicator/constant function on the whole unit disk is a highly simple, rotationally symmetric test function giving the critical necessary restriction exponent.
the full-disk constant datum is rotationally symmetric and gives the sharp critical exponent, obstructing lower-p restriction estimates
The all-ones disk function is a highly symmetric sharpness example whose extension forces the restriction exponent and rules out lower-p estimates.
The constant unit-disk function is the sharp full-support test forcing the restriction exponent threshold, with maximal rotational regularity but used to rule out stronger estimates.
The constant unit-disk amplitude is a trivial, maximally symmetric sharpness example showing the restriction exponent cannot be improved below the stated threshold.
The constant function on the disk gives the sharp integrability threshold for the restriction exponent, obstructing any estimate below it, and is a tight highly regular test case.
the constant, rotation-invariant test amplitude is a low-complexity sharpness example producing the critical restriction exponent rather than refuting the stated conjecture
the radially constant full-disk datum is the sharp stationary-phase obstruction giving the critical restriction exponent
The full constant datum is the symmetric sharpness test forcing the critical restriction exponent and obstructing stronger lower-p estimates.
The full-disk constant test function is rotationally symmetric and gives the sharp obstruction to lowering the restriction exponent.
The constant datum on the unit disk is the rotationally symmetric sharpness example forcing the critical restriction exponent.

examples.name: Two-delta shaded-tube enemy configuration
examples.note: The toy case where each tube carries two small shaded balls, possibly all clustered in one small ball unless a separation condition is imposed, visibly motivates the two-ends nonconcentration hypothesis and the Cauchy-Schwarz incidence gain.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker analyzes the two-delta shaded-tube configuration, comparing clustered versus separated shadings and deriving volume/counting bounds such as the Cauchy–Schwarz lower bound.

passes.reason: the shadings are collapsed into one tiny ball, giving a maximally concentrated bad case that shows an unqualified incidence lower bound fails
The shaded tubes with all two-delta shadings concentrated in one tiny ball are a collapsed worst-case enemy showing that an incidence lower bound is ill-posed without a two-ends assumption.
the shaded-tube configuration collapses all shadings into a tiny ball as a worst-case obstruction to any lower-bound without a two-ends assumption
The two-delta shading clustered inside one tiny ball is a collapsed worst-case configuration used as an enemy to show the unqualified incidence bound is ill-posed.
The two-delta shaded-tube configuration collapses each shading into a tiny common ball, giving an enemy case with minimal union volume that shows naive incidence bounds need a two-ends assumption.
The shaded pieces are collapsed into a tiny common ball, giving an extremally small-union enemy configuration that shows the unqualified incidence problem is ill posed.
The shaded tubes are deliberately clustered into a tiny ball to defeat naive incidence lower bounds, forming a collapsed worst-case configuration.
the shaded parts of many tubes are collapsed into one tiny ball, giving a near-minimal union volume and serving as the enemy case showing a lower-bound problem needs a two-ends hypothesis
the two shaded delta balls are deliberately collapsed into one tiny ball, minimizing the union and showing why a two-ends hypothesis is needed
The enemy shading collapses all tube mass into a tiny ball, an extremally concentrated degenerate configuration showing why a two-ends hypothesis is needed.
The enemy configuration collapses all two-delta shadings into one tiny ball, minimizing the union and obstructing any incidence bound without a two-ends assumption.
The shaded pieces are collapsed into one tiny ball, giving the worst clustered obstruction to an unqualified incidence lower bound.
